samples.text: All birds can fly. Penguins are birds that cannot fly. Does the statement 'all birds can fly' hold without exception?
The claim says every bird is able to fly. However, penguins are birds that are unable to fly. Is the original claim universally true?
A universal statement claims that all birds fly, but penguins are an exception because they do not fly. Is the universal statement completely correct?
The proposition asserts that every avian species possesses flight ability. Penguins are avian animals lacking flight capability. Is the proposition exception-free?

If a universal statement has even one counterexample, then the statement is not universally true. 'All birds can fly' has a counterexample because penguins cannot fly. Is the statement universally true?
All birds can fly. Penguins are birds that cannot fly. Many zoos have penguin exhibits. Does the statement 'all birds can fly' hold without exception?
